did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a deep learning approach involving a pre-trained AlexNet convolutional neural network (CNN) for automatic skin melanoma diagnosis. The model was fine-tuned using transfer learning, and its hyperparameters were optimized using the Marine Predators Algorithm (MPA). The study achieved an accuracy of 98.47% on the HAM-10000 dataset for classifying seven types of skin lesions.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and fine-tuning of a pre-trained AlexNet model optimized by the Marine Predators Algorithm (MPA) for skin cancer diagnosis. It describes specific methodology, utilizes the HAM-10000 dataset, and reports new experimental results, including an accuracy of 98.47%, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Transfer-learning based skin cancer diagnosis using fine-tuned AlexNet by marine predators algorithm
journal: IAES International Journal of Artificial Intelligence
brief_summary: This study addresses the critical need for early skin cancer diagnosis by proposing a transfer learning-based approach using a fine-tuned AlexNet convolutional neural network. The model's hyperparameters were optimized using the Marine Predators Algorithm (MPA) to enhance performance and prevent overfitting. The research utilized the HAM-10000 dataset, which contains 10,015 dermatoscopic images across seven classes of skin lesions. The evaluation metrics included accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score, with the dataset split into 70% for training and 30% for testing. The proposed MPA-AlexNet method achieved a high accuracy of 98.47%, outperforming several existing methods such as SVM, Random Forest, and DenseNet. The results demonstrate the effectiveness of integrating meta-heuristic optimization with deep learning for automated skin cancer classification.

paper_terms: Actinic keratoses and intraepithelial carcinoma/bowen disease
lesion_types_studied.details: Class 0 in the HAM-10000 dataset used for the seven-class classification experiment.
Class 1 in the HAM-10000 dataset used for the seven-class classification experiment.
Class 2 in the HAM-10000 dataset used for the seven-class classification experiment.
Class 3 in the HAM-10000 dataset used for the seven-class classification experiment.
Class 4 in the HAM-10000 dataset used for the seven-class classification experiment. Identified as the most dangerous skin cancer in the introduction.
Class 5 in the HAM-10000 dataset used for the seven-class classification experiment.
Class 6 in the HAM-10000 dataset used for the seven-class classification experiment.

all_task_types.target: Seven classes of skin lesions (Actinic keratoses, Basal cell carcinoma, Benign keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Melanoma, Melanocytic nevi, Vascular lesions)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multiclass classification of skin cancer using a fine-tuned AlexNet model optimized by the Marine Predators Algorithm (MPA) on the HAM-10000 dataset. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (lesion classes) rather than continuous values. Evaluation metrics include accuracy (98.47%), precision, recall, and F1-score, which are standard for classification tasks.

datasets_used.name: HAM-10000
datasets_used.description: A public dataset containing 10,015 dermatoscopic images of skin lesions gathered from patients in Australia and Austria. The images have dimensions of 600x450 pixels and are centered crops.
classes_used: Actinic keratoses and intraepithelial carcinoma/bowen disease

use.training: 70% of the dataset was used for training the fine-tuned AlexNet model.
use.testing: 30% of the dataset was used for testing and evaluating the network's efficacy.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. It compared the proposed method's accuracy (98.47%) qualitatively against existing methods without statistical significance analysis.

HAM-10000.notes: The proposed MPA-AlexNet method achieved superior performance compared to all listed baselines (SVM, KNN, DT, RF, CNN, DenseNet) across accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score. The loss decreased from 0.5 at 400 epochs to 0.15 at 1000 epochs. The ROC curve showed high True Positive Rate (TPR) and low False Positive Rate (FPR).

architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained AlexNet network with specified architecture and weights used for transfer learning

layers: Pooling layers (placed after each convolutional layer)
architecture_details.description: The network includes convolutional layers acting as filters to detect features, pooling layers to compress features and reduce spatial dimensions, and fully connected layers to capture higher-level features.

training_details.optimizer: Optimized via Marine Predators Algorithm (MPA) from options: sgdm, rmsprop, adam
training_details.loss_function: Loss function (cost) minimized via gradient descent

classification_or_output_head.output: Predicted labels for each image determining the detected class among 7 classes

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing images from 600x450 pixels to 227x227 pixels to match AlexNet input requirements
Normalizing pixel values to a standardized scale (e.g., [0,1])
conclusions: The proposed method, which combines a fine-tuned pre-trained AlexNet network with the Marine Predators Algorithm (MPA) for hyperparameter optimization, achieved a classification accuracy of 98.47% on the HAM-10000 dataset.

The integration of the MPA optimizer effectively adjusted hyperparameters (learning rate, mini-batch size, optimizer, kernel size, and validation frequency), which prevented network overfitting and enhanced model performance.
The fine-tuning approach demonstrated high learning ability on limited input images and contributed to decreasing diagnosis error.
The proposed MPA-AlexNet method outperformed existing methods, including SVM, KNN, Decision Tree, Random Forest, CNN, and DenseNet, in terms of accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score.

The study confirms the effectiveness of transfer learning and meta-heuristic optimization for automated skin cancer diagnosis, addressing the gap in classifying multiple classes of skin diseases simultaneously.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM-10000 dataset, which is geographically biased towards patients from Australia and Austria, limiting generalizability to other populations.
The paper does not report per-class performance metrics (precision, recall, F1-score) for the seven lesion classes, preventing assessment of performance on imbalanced or rare classes like Melanoma.
No external validation was performed; the model was only evaluated on a single internal 70/30 train-test split of the HAM-10000 dataset.
The paper does not report formal statistical significance tests (e.g., p-values) to validate the superiority of the proposed MPA-AlexNet method over baselines.

The comparison with baseline methods (SVM, KNN, etc.) uses results cited from previous literature ([29], [30]) rather than re-running these baselines on the same experimental setup, which may introduce unfair comparison due to differing preprocessing or evaluation protocols.
The paper does not specify the class distribution (sample counts per class) within the training and testing splits, raising potential concerns about class imbalance effects.
No data leakage mitigation strategies (e.g., patient-level splitting) are explicitly described, which is critical for dermatological datasets where multiple images may come from the same patient.
The study does not address clinical deployment challenges such as inference speed, computational resource requirements, or integration with clinical workflows.
The authors acknowledge that 'further and in-depth studies may be needed to confirm its efficient on more patient,' indicating limited evidence for broad clinical applicability.
